title: Native semantics before ARIA

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Native semantics before ARIA; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Native HTML controls provide built-in keyboard, focus, role, name, state, and form behavior that custom widgets must recreate completely.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A button element triggers an action and works with Enter and Space without a custom keydown implementation. Your goal is to replace unnecessary ARIA or generic clickable elements with native HTML controls. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Document foundations; Text and page semantics; Forms and native controls; Sections and landmarks; Labels and input controls. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Native HTML controls provide built-in keyboard, focus, role, name, state, and form behavior that custom widgets must recreate completely. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Native semantics still require labels and correct use, and ARIA can supplement but cannot repair invalid interaction design automatically. This draft therefore rejects: A clickable div with role=button is fully equivalent to button without any other code.

includes: Select native elements and add ARIA only to fill a genuine semantic gap without conflicting roles.
Replace unnecessary ARIA or generic clickable elements with native HTML controls.
excludes: Native semantics still require labels and correct use, and ARIA can supplement but cannot repair invalid interaction design automatically.

outcomes: Replace unnecessary ARIA or generic clickable elements with native HTML controls.

examples.title: Native semantics before ARIA: concrete case
examples.situation: A button element triggers an action and works with Enter and Space without a custom keydown implementation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A button element triggers an action and works with Enter and Space without a custom keydown implementation.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Prefer the native button for actions because role alone does not add keyboard, focus, disabled, or form behavior.
examples.result: The result follows because Prefer the native button for actions because role alone does not add keyboard, focus, disabled, or form behavior.

examples.title: Native semantics before ARIA: boundary case
examples.situation: Native semantics still require labels and correct use, and ARIA can supplement but cannot repair invalid interaction design automatically.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Native semantics still require labels and correct use, and ARIA can supplement but cannot repair invalid interaction design automatically.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: A clickable div with role=button is fully equivalent to button without any other code.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Prefer the native button for actions because role alone does not add keyboard, focus, disabled, or form behavior.

recap.summary: You should now be able to replace unnecessary ARIA or generic clickable elements with native HTML controls. Keep this boundary in view: Native semantics still require labels and correct use, and ARIA can supplement but cannot repair invalid interaction design automatically.
